entry.title: Check Point CVE-2026-50751 Exploitation, UniFi Root Chain, and NSO WhatsApp Phishing
entry.content:
This digest window was dominated by high-impact vulnerability reporting, including active exploitation of a Check Point VPN flaw and newly disclosed critical attack paths in UniFi OS Server and Gogs. Reporting also highlighted continued spyware-linked targeting of messaging users, Russia-aligned influence and surveillance activity, and several major data exposure incidents affecting healthcare, humanitarian, and consumer security services. Check Point warned of active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical logic-flow vulnerability affecting Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments using deprecated IKEv1 configurations. The flaw can allow unauthenticated remote attackers to bypass user authentication and establish VPN access without a valid user password under specific legacy configuration conditions. Check Point said suspicious activity was first observed on June 4, with earliest exploitation dating to May 7, and noted one post-exploitation case associated with a Qilin ransomware affiliate. Ubiquiti UniFi OS Server 5.0.6 and earlier was reported vulnerable to a chained unauthenticated root remote code execution path involving CVE-2026-34908, CVE-2026-34909, and CVE-2026-34910. Bishop Fox validated the chain against a live instance and described an authentication bypass caused by differences between raw URI validation and Nginx normalized URI routing, followed by command injection through a package-update endpoint and root escalation via passwordless sudo privileges. Gogs released version 0.14.3 to address a critical argument injection zero-day affecting all releases up to and including 0.14.2 and 0.15.0+dev. Rapid7 reported that authenticated non-admin users could exploit the flaw to compromise servers, access private repositories, steal credentials, move laterally, and tamper with hosted source code, with default configurations increasing exposure because open registration and unrestricted repository creation are enabled by default. Meta and WhatsApp accused NSO Group of conducting new spear-phishing activity against WhatsApp users in violation of a permanent injunction barring the company from using WhatsApp as an attack vector. The reported campaign used social engineering to drive victims to malicious external websites, and Meta said it removed associated test accounts and groups. Domains linked to the activity included fr24cast[.]com, ghazacast[.]com, and ikhwancast[.]com. Check Point Research also described a malware distribution ecosystem impersonating open-source tools and delivering RemusStealer, AnimateClipper, and a newly identified loader named SessionGate. In parallel, Dashlane disclosed an attack affecting fewer than 20 users in which threat actors brute-forced two-factor codes to register unauthorized devices and download encrypted password vaults. DentaQuest suffered a data breach after ShinyHunters leaked exfiltrated data tied to an estimated 2.6 million accounts. The exposed information reportedly included names, email addresses, government identifiers, and health insurance details, placing the incident within the broader pattern of healthcare and insurance-sector data theft. The United Nations World Food Programme disclosed unauthorized access to its Gaza self-registration application, affecting approximately 600,000 Palestinian households. Reported exposed data included names, identification numbers, mobile numbers, and location information, creating heightened sensitivity due to the humanitarian context and the population affected. Researchers reported that Kremlin-linked actors spent eight months targeting Armenia’s parliamentary election through the Matryoshka influence campaign. The activity used fabricated news reports, manipulated videos, bot amplification, fake media sites, impersonated journalists, and interconnected influence networks to promote narratives accusing Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan of corruption, anti-Russian plotting, and serious criminal conduct. Armenian authorities also reported false bomb threats sent to polling stations from foreign phone numbers and email addresses. Russia’s Ministry of Digital Development published updated technical standards for SORM, the country’s digital interception and surveillance system. The new rules expand searchable data categories to include passport information, tax identifiers, usernames, domains, URLs, device identifiers, corporate records, and geographic coordinates, while applying compliance burdens to telecom operators, cloud providers, hosting services, banks, universities, and large organizations operating internet infrastructure. SpecterOps published updated AzureHound least-privilege documentation that narrows required Microsoft Graph and Azure RBAC permissions. The validated configuration replaces broad permissions such as Directory.Read.All and RoleManagement.Read.All with eight granular Microsoft Graph application permissions and substitutes the built-in Azure Reader role with a custom AzureHound Reader role containing 16 specific read actions. BleepingComputer covered Wazuh Cloud as a managed, cloud-native version of the open-source Wazuh platform intended to reduce SIEM and XDR operational complexity. The service is described as handling backend maintenance, patches, rule updates, threat intelligence updates, scaling, preconfigured dashboards, and AI-generated weekly security assessments across endpoint, container, and cloud workloads.

Gogs patches critical zero-day enabling remote code execution — Must Read

Gogs has released version 0.14.3 to fix a critical zero-day argument-injection flaw that lets authenticated users gain remote code execution and access private repositories. Admins should upgrade immediately or restrict registration and repository creation to reduce exposure.

  • Gogs patched a critical argument injection zero-day vulnerability in version 0.14.3 released on June 7.
  • The flaw affects all Gogs releases up to and including 0.14.2 and 0.15.0+dev and has not yet been assigned a CVE ID.
  • Authenticated attackers without admin privileges can exploit the vulnerability to compromise a targeted server, read private repositories, steal credentials, move laterally, and alter hosted source code.
  • Rapid7 researcher Jonah Burgess said default Gogs configurations are affected because open registration and unlimited repository creation are enabled by default.
  • Rapid7 recommended immediate upgrades and, for users who cannot patch right away, restricting user registration and repository creation in app.ini.

Critical Check Point VPN Flaw Exploited to Bypass Passwords in IKEv1 Setups — Must Read

Check Point warns that attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-50751, a critical flaw in IKEv1-based Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access setups, to bypass password authentication and establish VPN sessions. Affected organizations should apply the hotfix or disable legacy IKEv1 configurations, as exploitation has already targeted dozens of organizations and is linked in at least one case to Qilin ransomware activity.

  • Check Point warned of active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical vulnerability affecting Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol.
  • CVE-2026-50751 is a logic flow weakness in certificate validation that can allow an unauthenticated remote attacker to bypass user authentication and establish a remote access VPN connection without a valid user password.
  • Successful exploitation requires Remote Access or Mobile Access to be enabled, IKEv1 to be enabled for remote access, legacy Remote Access clients to be accepted, and machine certificates not to be required for connections.
  • Check Point said it first observed suspicious activity on June 4, 2026, with the earliest observed exploitation dating back to May 7, 2026, and activity increasing this month.
  • Check Point said exploitation has been limited to a few dozen targeted organizations globally, and one post-exploitation case was associated with a Qilin ransomware affiliate.

Critical UniFi OS bug lets hackers gain root without authentication — Should Read

Three patched UniFi OS Server flaws can be chained to gain unauthenticated remote code execution and escalate to root, giving attackers full control of the network management appliance. Organizations should upgrade to UniFi OS Server 5.0.8 or later and verify systems were not already compromised.

  • Attackers can chain CVE-2026-34908, CVE-2026-34909, and CVE-2026-34910 in Ubiquiti UniFi OS Server 5.0.6 and earlier to execute remote code with root privileges without authentication.
  • Bishop Fox researchers validated the complete attack path on a live UniFi OS Server 5.0.6 instance but did not release full exploit details or a working proof of concept.
  • The authentication bypass stems from a mismatch where UniFi OS validates the raw request URI while Nginx routes requests based on a normalized URI.
  • The exploit chain reaches a package-update endpoint where unvalidated input enables command injection, and the affected service account’s passwordless sudo privileges make root escalation trivial.
  • Bishop Fox released a free detection script, and the researchers confirmed the attack chain does not work on UniFi OS Server 5.0.8.

Armenia’s pro-Europe party wins election despite Russia-linked disinformation — Should Read

Armenia’s pro-European ruling party won the parliamentary election despite a months-long Russia-linked disinformation campaign involving fake media, manipulated videos, bots and hoax bomb threats. The result signals continued public support for Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan’s shift away from Moscow and toward Western partners.

  • Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan declared victory after his pro-European Civil Contract party won nearly 50% of the parliamentary election vote.
  • Researchers said Kremlin-linked actors spent eight months targeting Armenia’s election through the Matryoshka campaign, using fabricated news reports, manipulated videos and bot activity.
  • The campaign accused Pashinyan of corruption, plotting against Russia and involvement in crimes including sexual assault and organ trafficking.
  • Armenian authorities said false bomb threats were sent to several polling stations from foreign phone numbers and email addresses, possibly as attempts at hybrid influence.
  • Researchers at the Institute for Strategic Dialogue said Russia-aligned networks created fake media websites, impersonated journalists and legitimate news outlets, and amplified false narratives through influencers and interconnected websites.

Meta Blocks NSO Group's New WhatsApp Phishing Attack, Files Contempt Order — Should Read

Meta says it blocked new NSO Group-linked WhatsApp spear-phishing attempts that used malicious links to lure users outside the app. The company is also seeking a federal contempt order, alleging NSO violated an injunction barring it from targeting WhatsApp and its users.

  • Meta said it detected and blocked spear-phishing attempts linked to Israeli spyware vendor NSO Group.
  • Meta is filing a federal court contempt order against NSO Group for allegedly violating a permanent injunction that barred it from targeting WhatsApp and its users.
  • Meta said the campaign tried to trick people into clicking malicious links that led to external websites outside WhatsApp, similar to previously reported one-click phishing campaigns linked to NSO Group.
  • Meta said it found NSO Group creating test accounts and groups on WhatsApp, which Meta has since taken down.
  • The malicious domains Meta linked to the activity were fr24cast[.]com, ghazacast[.]com, and ikhwancast[.]com.

Russia upgrades rules for its digital spy system to better track citizens online — Should Read

Russia has updated SORM rules to make its digital surveillance system faster and more automated, expanding the data authorities can search and correlate across telecom and internet infrastructure. The changes could deepen state monitoring of citizens’ online activity and pressure smaller providers that cannot afford compliance.

  • Russia’s Ministry of Digital Development published updated technical standards for SORM, the country’s System for Operative Investigative Activities, at the end of May.
  • The new SORM rules specify how information must be searched, processed and transmitted, going beyond earlier requirements for operators to install equipment and provide access to authorized agencies.
  • The updated regulations expand searchable data to include names, passport information, tax identification numbers, addresses, usernames, domains, URLs, corporate records, device identifiers and geographic coordinates.
  • Digital rights advocates cited in the article said the changes could strengthen the government’s ability to monitor online activity and encourage self-censorship while keeping internet services online.
  • The regulations may impose costly compliance burdens on telecom providers, hosting providers, data centers, cloud operators, major technology companies, banks, universities and large corporations that manage their own internet infrastructure.

Keeping a Short Leash: New AzureHound Least-Privilege Documentation — May Read

SpecterOps has published and validated new least-privilege permission guidance for AzureHound, replacing broad default access with granular Microsoft Graph and Azure RBAC permissions. The main takeaway is that AzureHound can collect equivalent BloodHound data with much narrower read-only access, though teams must review permissions when upgrading collectors.

  • SpecterOps published new AzureHound least-privilege documentation, updated manual deployment steps, and updated scripted configuration that use a narrower permission set by default.
  • AzureHound previously required broad Microsoft Graph permissions, the Azure RBAC Reader role at the Tenant Root Management Group, and the Entra ID Directory Readers role.
  • The validated least-privilege Microsoft Graph configuration uses eight granular application permissions instead of Directory.Read.All and RoleManagement.Read.All.
  • The validated least-privilege Azure RBAC configuration replaces the built-in Reader role with a custom AzureHound Reader role containing 16 specific read actions.
  • SpecterOps validated the least-privilege configuration through source inventory, endpoint-level permission testing, and full AzureHound output comparison against the previous broad-permission configuration.

8th June – Threat Intelligence Report — Optional

Check Point’s weekly threat report highlights major data breaches, emerging AI-enabled attack techniques, and several actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting Android, Cisco, SolarWinds Serv-U, and Windows Netlogon. Organizations should prioritize patching exploited flaws and watch for supply-chain compromise, account takeover, and malware distribution campaigns.

  • DentaQuest suffered a data breach after ShinyHunters leaked exfiltrated data, exposing an estimated 2.6 million accounts with names, emails, government IDs, and health insurance details.
  • Dashlane disclosed an attack in which threat actors brute-forced two-factor codes to register unauthorized devices and download encrypted password vaults for fewer than 20 users.
  • The United Nations World Food Programme disclosed unauthorized access to its Gaza self-registration application, affecting about 600,000 Palestinian households and exposing names, identification numbers, mobile numbers, and location data.
  • Google, Cisco, SolarWinds, and Microsoft addressed or reported exploitation of vulnerabilities affecting Android, Unified Communications Manager, Serv-U, and Windows Netlogon.
  • Check Point Research investigated a malware distribution ecosystem that impersonated open-source tools and led to infections by RemusStealer, AnimateClipper, and a new loader called SessionGate.

Reducing security operations complexity with Wazuh Cloud — Optional

Wazuh Cloud is presented as a managed SIEM/XDR platform that reduces security operations complexity by handling infrastructure, scaling, maintenance, and alert analysis. Its main pitch is helping teams gain faster visibility and reduce analyst workload through automation and AI-assisted security insights.

  • The article says security operations teams face challenges including lengthy SIEM/XDR deployments, ongoing maintenance demands, high alert volumes, scaling constraints, inflexible licensing models, and limited support.
  • Wazuh Cloud is described as a fully managed, cloud-native version of the open source Wazuh platform that handles backend operations, patches, rule enhancements, threat intelligence updates, and version upgrades.
  • Wazuh Cloud supports lightweight Wazuh agents across Windows, Linux, macOS, containers, and cloud workloads, with pre-configured rules and dashboards enabled immediately.
  • The Wazuh AI Security Analyst analyzes security alerts, vulnerability data, and endpoint activity to generate weekly assessments, trends, high-risk highlights, and prioritized remediation recommendations.
  • The article states that Wazuh Cloud dynamically scales resources based on agent volume and data ingestion rates and offers flexible tiers based on agent count, data retention, and module needs.
entry.summary: This digest window was dominated by high-impact vulnerability reporting, including active exploitation of a Check Point VPN flaw and newly disclosed critical attack paths in UniFi OS Server and Gogs. Reporting also highlighted continued spyware-linked targeting of messaging users, Russia-aligned...

entry.title: CVE-2026-50751 VPN Exploitation, Qilin Links, and VerdantBamboo Appliance Intrusions
entry.content:
During this coverage window, reporting centered on edge-device compromise, identity-focused intrusion paths, and the operational pressure created by AI-enabled threat activity. The most consequential items involved Check Point VPN zero-day exploitation tied to Qilin ransomware activity and VerdantBamboo’s use of BRICKSTORM against Linux, BSD, and appliance environments. Check Point released updates for CVE-2026-50751, a critical vulnerability affecting certain Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments. The flaw allows unauthenticated remote attackers to bypass authentication and establish a remote access VPN connection against targeted Mobile Access or SSL VPNs, Remote Access VPNs, or Spark firewalls. Check Point said exploitation began on May 7, increased sharply in early June, affected only a few dozen organizations globally, and included at least one incident linked to the Qilin ransomware operation. The affected Check Point configurations are limited to deployments using the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol with gateways that accept legacy Remote Access clients and do not require a machine certificate. Check Point also disclosed CVE-2026-50752, a certificate validation issue in deprecated IKEv1 that could support man-in-the-middle attacks against site-to-site VPN connections, though the company reported no evidence of exploitation in the wild. Separately, Volexity attributed a multi-stage appliance-focused intrusion to the China-nexus cluster VerdantBamboo, which overlaps with Clay Typhoon, UNC5221, and Warp Panda. The actor compromised an Egnyte Storage Sync system in September 2025 by exploiting a local privilege escalation flaw and deploying BRICKSTORM, then used the malware’s proxying functions and compromised credentials to access Microsoft 365. Volexity later found the same victim had been breached again through stolen administrative credentials used to connect to a firewall, configure web SSL VPN access, and deploy additional malware to a Synology NAS appliance; investigation also identified a compromised managed services provider with a pfSense firewall infected by a BSD variant of BRICKSTORM. Multiple reports highlighted credential-centric risk, ranging from AI-assisted phishing to incidents involving legitimate cloud or SaaS infrastructure. The Hacker News described how AI-generated phishing increases Tier 1 SOC alert volume by enabling more convincing lures, personalized messages, fake login pages, short-lived domains, and harder-to-triage variations. The article focused on sandbox-based investigation of suspicious links and phishing chains, including automated navigation, CAPTCHA handling, behavioral indicators, IOCs, MITRE ATT&CK mapping, and analyst handoff artifacts. IRONSCALES reported a credential-harvest case involving Microsoft’s Transport Message Recall Routing Agent, where an email appeared authenticated as internal with AuthAs=Internal and AuthMechanism=05. The message appeared as a Message Recall Report from Office365Reports@microsoft.com and linked to a legitimate outlook.office.com recall URL, but the body included a credential prompt that the article said was not standard language for a Microsoft recall notification. Themis classified the message at 72% confidence with a VIP Recipient label, citing a first-time sender, elevated risk, and anomalous community intelligence; four mailboxes were quarantined. Oxford University disclosed a data breach after third-party provider Group GTI reported compromise of the CareerConnect careers platform. Attackers accessed first names, last names, email addresses, and encrypted passwords for users who do not authenticate through Single Sign-On after breaching the platform on May 28. Oxford said there was no evidence that course information, uploaded files, appointment data, financial information, students’ passwords, or university systems were compromised, and warned users of possible phishing or scam emails following the incident. Several articles framed AI as both an attacker accelerator and a source of operational strain for defenders. Coverage from the 2026 Gartner Security and Risk Summit described AI’s role in compliance automation and threat detection, while also emphasizing attacker use cases such as phishing, exploit discovery, prompt injection, and hijacking of agentic automation. Gartner warned that by 2027, 90% of organizations using large language models primarily to reduce false positives and costs would miss their objectives to reduce breach rates. The summit coverage also described a shift in exposure management toward validation of reachability, exploitability, and attack paths rather than reliance on dashboards or AI tooling alone. Gartner characterized the market as moving toward convergence between Exposure Assessment Platforms and Adversarial Exposure Validation, potentially forming a Unified Exposure Management Platform category. The discussion also referenced a dedicated Mobilization Coordinator role and a forecast that organizations adopting it by 2030 could reduce critical exposure dwell time by up to 60%. A separate essay, “The Hardest Fork,” argued that AI-assisted vulnerability discovery could overwhelm existing open source security processes. The author described Mythos as capable of combining scanner findings into more serious exploit chains and asserted that coordinated vulnerability disclosure was built for a lower-volume vulnerability environment. The piece proposed a trusted disclosure coordination model and, where upstream projects cannot patch quickly, a sustainably funded neutral “maintainer of last resort” for critical forks.

Check Point links VPN zero-day attacks to Qilin ransomware gang — Must Read

Check Point patched a critical zero-day authentication bypass in certain Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments using deprecated IKEv1 after limited real-world exploitation, including activity tied to a Qilin ransomware affiliate. Customers should apply updates immediately or disable legacy IKEv1 configurations and require stronger certificate-based authentication.

  • Check Point released security updates for CVE-2026-50751, a critical flaw affecting Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments that was exploited in zero-day attacks.
  • CVE-2026-50751 allows unauthenticated remote attackers to bypass authentication on targeted Mobile Access or SSL VPNs, Remote Access VPNs, or Spark firewalls and establish a remote access VPN connection.
  • The vulnerability affects only deployments using the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol with security gateways that accept legacy Remote Access clients and do not require a machine certificate for connections.
  • Check Point said exploitation began on May 7, surged in early June, affected only a few dozen organizations worldwide, and included at least one incident linked to the Qilin ransomware operation.
  • Check Point also disclosed CVE-2026-50752, a certificate validation vulnerability in deprecated IKEv1 that could be exploited in man-in-the-middle attacks on site-to-site VPN connections, but said it has not found evidence of in-the-wild exploitation.

VerdantBamboo Deploys BSD Variant of BRICKSTORM on Linux Appliances — Must Read

China-linked VerdantBamboo compromised Linux and BSD-based appliances, including Egnyte Storage Sync, pfSense, and Synology NAS, using BRICKSTORM, PLENET, and AGENTPSD to maintain stealthy access and pivot into Microsoft 365. The campaign highlights the group’s focus on edge and storage devices that often lack EDR coverage, using MSP compromise and stolen credentials to evade defenses.

  • Volexity attributed the activity to the China-nexus threat cluster VerdantBamboo, which overlaps with groups tracked as Clay Typhoon, UNC5221, and Warp Panda.
  • Volexity discovered in September 2025 that VerdantBamboo had compromised an unnamed victim's Egnyte Storage Sync system by exploiting a local privilege escalation flaw to deploy BRICKSTORM.
  • The attackers used BRICKSTORM's proxying capabilities on the Storage Sync system, along with compromised credentials, to access the victim's Microsoft 365 environment.
  • VerdantBamboo later breached the same organization using stolen administrative credentials to connect to the firewall, configure web SSL VPN access, and deploy additional malware to a Synology NAS appliance.
  • Further investigation found that the threat actor had compromised the victim organization's managed services provider and infected the MSP's pfSense firewall with a BSD variant of BRICKSTORM.

Microsoft's Own Message Recall Agent Delivered a Credential Harvest — Should Read

A phishing attempt abused Microsoft’s own Message Recall mechanism to generate an internally authenticated recall report that appeared trusted but included a prompt to enter email credentials. The key risk is that system-generated internal messages can bypass normal external email defenses, so detection must inspect content and context rather than relying on authentication alone.

  • Microsoft's Transport Message Recall Routing Agent generated an email authenticated as Internal with AuthAs=Internal and AuthMechanism=05, and the message direction was Originating.
  • The message appeared as a Message Recall Report from Office365Reports@microsoft.com and linked to a legitimate outlook.office.com message recall URL.
  • The email body included the credential prompt, “If prompted, login with your email address and password,” which the article states is not standard language in a Microsoft message recall notification.
  • Themis classified the message at 72% confidence with a VIP Recipient label, citing signals including a first-time sender, high risk, and anomalous community intelligence.
  • Four mailboxes were quarantined after the message was detected.

Oxford University discloses data breach after careers platform hack — May Read

Oxford University disclosed a data breach after third-party provider Group GTI’s CareerConnect platform was hacked, exposing users’ names, email addresses, and encrypted passwords for non-SSO accounts. Oxford says its own systems were not compromised, but affected users may face phishing attempts.

  • Oxford University disclosed a data breach after third-party provider Group GTI informed it that the CareerConnect career services platform had been compromised.
  • Attackers breached the CareerConnect platform on May 28 and accessed users’ first names, last names, email addresses, and encrypted passwords for users who do not sign in using Single Sign-On.
  • Oxford University said there is no evidence that course information, uploaded files, appointment information, financial information, students’ passwords, or university systems were compromised.
  • Group GTI invalidated locally set CareerConnect passwords, and affected alumni, research staff, and employer users will be asked to reset their passwords when they next sign in.
  • Oxford University warned staff, students, and external CareerConnect users that they might be targeted by phishing or scam emails following the incident.

The Hardest Fork — Optional

The author argues that AI-driven vulnerability discovery will overwhelm today’s open source disclosure and patching systems, making current consumption models unsafe. The proposed solution is a coordinated disclosure pipeline plus a trusted “maintainer of last resort” to fork, patch, and distribute critical projects when upstream maintainers cannot keep up.

  • The author says Mythos is real and describes it as a different category of threat because it can combine many issues found by scanners into more serious exploit chains.
  • The article argues that the current model for consuming open source software is fundamentally broken and cannot be fixed quickly through incremental improvements.
  • The author says coordinated vulnerability disclosure was designed for a world with fewer serious vulnerabilities and will not keep up when models can find hundreds overnight in long-tail open source projects.
  • The article proposes a Plan A of one trusted, coordinated disclosure effort that routes vetted reports and patches upstream while supporting maintainers who want help.
  • The article proposes a Plan B involving a sustainably funded, staffed, neutral, and trusted “maintainer of last resort” to maintain forks when upstream projects cannot or will not patch in time.

Initial Reactions and Key Takeaways from the 2026 Gartner Security and Risk Summit — Optional

At the 2026 Gartner Security and Risk Summit, the central takeaway was that AI is accelerating both attacker and defender capabilities, making continuous exposure management and attack-path-based prioritization essential. Gartner and XM Cyber emphasized that organizations must focus less on more AI tools and more on proving which exposures are truly exploitable and tied to business risk.

  • The 2026 Gartner Security and Risk Summit highlighted AI as both a defensive enabler for tasks like compliance automation and threat detection and an attacker advantage for phishing, exploit discovery, prompt injections, and agentic automation hijacks.
  • Gartner warned that by 2027, 90% of organizations using large language models primarily to reduce false positives and costs will miss their objectives to lower breach rates.
  • The article argues that effective exposure management depends on reachability, exploitability, and attack path validation rather than simply adopting more AI tools or dashboards.
  • Gartner described a market shift in which Exposure Assessment Platforms are converging with Adversarial Exposure Validation capabilities into a potential Unified Exposure Management Platform category.
  • Gartner recommended a dedicated Mobilization Coordinator role and predicted that organizations implementing it by 2030 will reduce critical exposure dwell time by up to 60%.

AI Phishing Is Crushing SOCs with Alert Volume: How to Reduce Tier 1 Overload — Optional

AI-generated phishing is increasing SOC alert volume and making Tier 1 triage harder, so the article argues teams need faster behavior-based analysis, automation, and structured reports to reduce escalations and response delays. It promotes ANY.RUN’s sandbox as a way to expose phishing chains quickly and cut Tier 1 workload.

  • AI enables attackers to create convincing phishing emails, fake login pages, and tailored lures quickly, increasing the number of alerts Tier 1 SOC analysts must review.
  • AI-driven phishing causes more lure variations, better impersonation, personalized messages, short-lived domains, and uncertain cases that are harder for Tier 1 teams to dismiss quickly.
  • The article says ANY.RUN’s Interactive Sandbox lets teams open suspicious links in an isolated browser environment and trace the full phishing chain without risking company devices or infrastructure.
  • ANY.RUN’s sandbox can automate steps such as navigating pages, solving CAPTCHAs, and triggering hidden phishing content while still allowing analysts to interact with sessions when needed.
  • The article says ANY.RUN’s Tier 1 Report provides verdicts, IOCs, behavioral indicators, MITRE ATT&CK mapping, AI summaries, and recommendations to speed Tier 1-to-Tier 2 handoffs.
entry.summary: During this coverage window, reporting centered on edge-device compromise, identity-focused intrusion paths, and the operational pressure created by AI-enabled threat activity. The most consequential items involved Check Point VPN zero-day exploitation tied to Qilin ransomware activity and...

entry.title: Hades PyPI Campaign, UNC3753 Extortion, and Meta Instagram Recovery Abuse
entry.content:
This digest highlights a coverage window dominated by software supply chain compromise, identity-centric intrusion, and abuse of trusted support or administrative workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on the Hades PyPI campaign, which combined cross-platform memory scraping, GitHub-based command-and-control, and prompt-injection intended to mislead automated analysis. Separately, reporting on UNC3753 and Meta’s Instagram account recovery breach illustrates how attackers continue to exploit human trust, support processes, and identity exposure to accelerate data theft and account compromise. StepSecurity reported that version 0.8.101 of the PyPI package ensmallen and related computational biology, bioinformatics, and genotype-phenotype packages were compromised with a shared malicious payload. The malware used a Python import hook to download a standalone Bun runtime and execute a companion index.js payload once per boot cycle, tracked through a /tmp/.bunran flag file. The Hades campaign payload included Linux, macOS, and Windows memory scrapers designed to read GitHub Actions Runner.Worker process memory and extract plaintext variables and tokens. Reporting also described GitHub-based command-and-control, encrypted exfiltration repositories, worm propagation through SSH/SCP and package publishing workflows, and persistence mechanisms that included a GitHub token revocation wiper deterrent. The same report noted a prompt-injection block inside the JavaScript bundle intended to influence automated LLM-based security analysis into classifying the package as benign. In related developer ecosystem news, Microsoft announced that Visual Studio Code 1.123 will delay automatic extension updates by two hours to limit exposure to supply chain attacks, while still allowing manual immediate updates and exempting trusted publishers such as Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI. Similar time-based installation delay controls have also appeared across Bundler, Bun, npm, pnpm, and Yarn. Google Mandiant and Google Threat Intelligence Group attributed a financially motivated U.S. data theft extortion campaign to UNC3753, targeting professional, legal, and financial services organizations between January and May 2026. The actor used vishing and social engineering to impersonate IT support, move victims into screen-sharing sessions, and guide installation of legitimate remote monitoring and management tools including AnyDesk, Bomgar, SuperOps RMM, and Zoho Assist. The FBI also reported that UNC3753 conducted physical intrusions by posing as IT technicians to enter corporate offices and steal data using external hard drives or USB drives. Reported stolen data included proprietary legal agreements, personally identifiable information, financial records, tax filings, audits, corporate client agreements, and Social Security numbers. Google said many operations moved from initial contact to extortion within a single business day, with searches, staging, and theft beginning in under an hour. Meta disclosed that more than 20,000 Instagram users had their accounts hijacked after attackers abused its AI-powered High Touch Support account recovery tool to reset passwords. The flaw allowed attackers to obtain password reset links because the tool did not verify whether email addresses were associated with the targeted Instagram accounts. Meta said affected data may have included contact information, dates of birth, posts, photos, videos, stories, direct messages, account activity, profile information, and linked accounts or services. Constella described executive identity exposure as a board-level issue, noting that attackers increasingly target CEOs, CFOs, board members, and senior leaders for their authority, influence, and trust relationships. The reporting emphasized that executive exposure often extends beyond enterprise systems into personal email, professional networking platforms, social media, public records, conference appearances, board memberships, and media coverage. The article highlighted growth in executive impersonation through fake emails, deepfake audio and video, fraudulent payment requests, and investor or vendor fraud. It framed executive compromise as a source of financial, operational, reputational, regulatory, legal, and strategic risk, with organizations increasingly tracking exposure rates, high-risk executive identities, impersonation incidents, remediation timing, and exposure trends.

The Hades Campaign: Graph ML PyPI Packages Deploy Cross-Platform Memory Scrapers, AI Analyst Misdirection, and a Wiper Deterrent — Must Read

A sophisticated PyPI supply-chain campaign dubbed Hades compromised multiple scientific and graph ML packages, using a Bun-based payload to steal credentials, scrape GitHub Actions runner memory across Linux, macOS, and Windows, spread through CI/CD and developer environments, and maintain GitHub-based C2. The malware also includes AI-analysis prompt injection and a token-revocation wiper deterrent, making immediate detection and incident response more difficult.

  • Version 0.8.101 of the PyPI package ensmallen and several related computational biology, bioinformatics, and genotype-phenotype packages were identified as compromised with the same malicious payload.
  • The malicious Python import hook downloads a standalone Bun runtime and executes a companion _index.js payload once per boot cycle using a /tmp/.bun_ran flag file.
  • The _index.js bundle contains a prompt-injection block intended to mislead automated LLM-based security analysis into classifying the package as clean.
  • The malware includes Linux, macOS, and Windows memory scrapers designed to read GitHub Actions Runner.Worker process memory and extract plaintext variables and tokens.
  • The campaign uses GitHub-based command-and-control channels, encrypted exfiltration repositories, worm propagation through SSH/SCP and package publishing workflows, and persistence services including a GitHub token revocation wiper deterrent.

UNC3753 Used Vishing and Physical Intrusions in U.S. Data Theft Extortion Campaign — Must Read

UNC3753 is using vishing, screen-sharing sessions, legitimate remote access tools, and even in-person impersonation of IT staff to steal sensitive data from U.S. legal, financial, and professional services firms. The group moves quickly from initial contact to exfiltration and extortion, often completing the operation within a single business day.

  • Google Mandiant and Google Threat Intelligence Group attributed a financially motivated data theft extortion campaign targeting U.S. professional, legal, and financial services organizations between January and May 2026 to UNC3753.
  • UNC3753 uses vishing and social engineering to pose as IT support, persuade victims to join screen-sharing sessions, and guide them to install legitimate remote monitoring and management tools such as AnyDesk, Bomgar, SuperOps RMM, or Zoho Assist.
  • The FBI reported that UNC3753 has also conducted physical intrusions by posing as IT technicians to enter corporate offices and steal data using external hard drives or USB drives.
  • Stolen data includes proprietary legal agreements, personally identifiable information, financial records, tax filings, audits, corporate client agreements, and Social Security numbers.
  • Google said many UNC3753 operations progressed from initial contact to data extortion within a single business day, with data searches, staging, and theft beginning in under an hour.

Over 20,000 Instagram accounts stolen in Meta AI support hack — Should Read

Meta says attackers abused a flaw in Instagram’s AI-assisted High Touch Support account recovery tool to reset passwords and hijack more than 20,000 accounts that lacked 2FA. Meta disabled the tool, invalidated reset links, secured affected accounts, and plans to add proper email verification before relaunching it.

  • Meta revealed that over 20,000 Instagram users had their accounts hijacked after attackers abused its AI-powered High Touch Support account recovery tool to reset passwords.
  • The flaw allowed attackers to obtain password reset links because the High Touch Support tool did not verify whether email addresses were associated with the targeted Instagram accounts.
  • Meta said in a breach letter that it discovered the vulnerability on May 31, 2026, and that Maine’s filing lists April 17 as the breach date.
  • Meta disabled the High Touch Support system and all password reset links it generated, enrolled potentially stolen accounts into a mandatory security checkpoint, and required affected users to reset passwords and re-authenticate.
  • Meta said attackers may have accessed affected users’ contact information, dates of birth, posts, photos, videos, stories, direct messages, account activity, profile information, and linked accounts or services.

VS Code Adds 2-Hour Extension Auto-Update Delay to Limit Supply Chain Attacks — Should Read

Microsoft is adding a two-hour delay to automatic VS Code extension updates to reduce exposure to malicious or compromised releases in software supply chain attacks. Users can still manually update immediately, and trusted publishers like Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI are exempt from the delay.

  • Microsoft announced that Visual Studio Code will delay automatic extension updates by two hours to help address software supply chain threats.
  • The delayed automatic update feature is available starting in Visual Studio Code 1.123.
  • Users can still manually update any extension immediately by using the Update button.
  • The two-hour delay does not apply to extensions from trusted publishers such as Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI, which will continue to update immediately.
  • Similar time-based installation delay controls have been added to Bundler, Bun, npm, pnpm, and Yarn.

Executive Identity Exposure Has Become a Board-Level Issue — Optional

Executive identities are increasingly targeted because their authority, trust, and public visibility make them ideal for impersonation, fraud, and broader compromise. Organizations should treat executive identity exposure as an enterprise risk and monitor, measure, and reduce it proactively.

  • Attackers increasingly target executives such as CEOs, CFOs, board members, and senior leaders because they possess influence, authority, and trust rather than necessarily the most technical access.
  • Executive identity exposure extends beyond corporate systems to personal email accounts, professional networking platforms, social media profiles, public records, conference appearances, board memberships, and media coverage.
  • Executive impersonation is increasing as attackers use publicly available information to mimic executives through fake emails, deepfake audio and video, fraudulent payment requests, and investor or vendor fraud.
  • The article states that executive identity exposure has become a board-level issue because executive compromise can create financial, operational, reputational, regulatory, legal, and strategic risks.
  • Organizations can measure and manage executive identity risk through metrics such as executive exposure rate, high-risk executive identities, impersonation incidents, time-to-remediation, and exposure trend analysis.
entry.summary: This digest highlights a coverage window dominated by software supply chain compromise, identity-centric intrusion, and abuse of trusted support or administrative workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on the Hades PyPI campaign, which combined cross-platform memory scraping,...

entry.title: VerdantBamboo BRICKSTORM, UNC3753 Extortion, PyPI Hades, and Meta Instagram Takeovers
entry.content:
Across this coverage window, reporting centered on identity-driven compromise, software supply chain abuse, and adversaries exploiting trust in legitimate platforms and support workflows. Several incidents involved attackers using valid credentials, recovery mechanisms, third-party services, or trusted software channels to accelerate access and reduce detection friction. Volexity attributed appliance-focused activity to the China-nexus cluster VerdantBamboo, overlapping with Clay Typhoon, UNC5221, and Warp Panda. The actor reportedly compromised an Egnyte Storage Sync system in September 2025 via local privilege escalation, deployed BRICKSTORM, and used the appliance’s proxying capabilities alongside compromised credentials to access the victim’s Microsoft 365 environment. The same organization was later breached again using stolen administrative credentials to connect to a firewall, configure web SSL VPN access, and deploy additional malware to a Synology NAS appliance. Volexity also found the victim’s managed services provider had been compromised, including infection of the MSP’s pfSense firewall with a BSD variant of BRICKSTORM, underscoring the actor’s focus on edge and storage appliances as durable access points. Google Mandiant and Google Threat Intelligence Group attributed a U.S. data theft extortion campaign targeting professional, legal, and financial services organizations between January and May 2026 to UNC3753. The group used vishing to impersonate IT support, guided victims into screen-sharing sessions, and installed legitimate RMM tools such as AnyDesk, Bomgar, SuperOps RMM, and Zoho Assist; the FBI also reported physical intrusions in which actors posed as IT technicians and stole data using removable media. Google said some operations moved from first contact to extortion within a single business day, with data searches, staging, and theft beginning in under an hour. StepSecurity reported the “Hades” campaign involving compromised PyPI packages including version 0.8.101 of ensmallen and related computational biology, bioinformatics, and genotype-phenotype packages. The malicious import hook downloaded a standalone Bun runtime and executed a companion JavaScript payload once per boot cycle, while the bundle included a prompt-injection block intended to mislead automated LLM-based analysis into classifying the package as benign. The malware included Linux, macOS, and Windows memory scrapers targeting GitHub Actions Runner.Worker process memory for plaintext variables and tokens. Reporting also described GitHub-based command-and-control, encrypted exfiltration repositories, worm-like propagation through SSH/SCP and package publishing workflows, and persistence services that included a GitHub token revocation wiper deterrent. Microsoft separately announced that Visual Studio Code 1.123 will delay automatic extension updates by two hours to reduce exposure to fast-moving extension supply chain attacks. Manual extension updates remain available immediately, and the delay does not apply to trusted publishers such as Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI; similar time-based controls have also appeared across Bundler, Bun, npm, pnpm, and Yarn. Meta disclosed that more than 20,000 Instagram users had accounts hijacked after attackers abused an AI-assisted High Touch Support account recovery tool. The flaw allowed password reset links to be generated without verifying that email addresses were associated with targeted accounts, enabling takeovers where two-factor authentication was not enabled; Meta said potentially exposed data included contact information, dates of birth, posts, media, stories, direct messages, account activity, profile information, and linked services. Oxford University disclosed a breach tied to the third-party Group GTI CareerConnect platform after attackers accessed users’ names, email addresses, and encrypted passwords for accounts not using Single Sign-On. Oxford said it had no evidence that course data, uploaded files, appointment information, financial data, students’ passwords, or university systems were compromised, while Group GTI invalidated locally set CareerConnect passwords. IRONSCALES described a Microsoft Transport Message Recall Routing Agent email that was authenticated as internal and appeared as a Message Recall Report from Office365Reports@microsoft.com, linking to a legitimate outlook.office.com recall URL. The body included a credential prompt that the report stated is not standard for Microsoft recall notifications, and four mailboxes were quarantined after detection. Constella separately highlighted executive identity exposure as a board-level issue, citing increased targeting of CEOs, CFOs, board members, and senior leaders through impersonation, deepfake audio and video, fraudulent payment requests, and AI-assisted personalization.

The Hades Campaign: Graph ML PyPI Packages Deploy Cross-Platform Memory Scrapers, AI Analyst Misdirection, and a Wiper Deterrent — Must Read

A sophisticated PyPI supply-chain campaign dubbed Hades compromised multiple scientific and graph ML packages, using a Bun-based payload to steal credentials, scrape GitHub Actions runner memory across Linux, macOS, and Windows, spread through CI/CD and developer environments, and maintain GitHub-based C2. The malware also includes AI-analysis prompt injection and a token-revocation wiper deterrent, making immediate detection and incident response more difficult.

  • Version 0.8.101 of the PyPI package ensmallen and several related computational biology, bioinformatics, and genotype-phenotype packages were identified as compromised with the same malicious payload.
  • The malicious Python import hook downloads a standalone Bun runtime and executes a companion _index.js payload once per boot cycle using a /tmp/.bun_ran flag file.
  • The _index.js bundle contains a prompt-injection block intended to mislead automated LLM-based security analysis into classifying the package as clean.
  • The malware includes Linux, macOS, and Windows memory scrapers designed to read GitHub Actions Runner.Worker process memory and extract plaintext variables and tokens.
  • The campaign uses GitHub-based command-and-control channels, encrypted exfiltration repositories, worm propagation through SSH/SCP and package publishing workflows, and persistence services including a GitHub token revocation wiper deterrent.

VerdantBamboo Deploys BSD Variant of BRICKSTORM on Linux Appliances — Must Read

China-linked VerdantBamboo compromised Linux and BSD-based appliances, including Egnyte Storage Sync, pfSense, and Synology NAS, using BRICKSTORM, PLENET, and AGENTPSD to maintain stealthy access and pivot into Microsoft 365. The campaign highlights the group’s focus on edge and storage devices that often lack EDR coverage, using MSP compromise and stolen credentials to evade defenses.

  • Volexity attributed the activity to the China-nexus threat cluster VerdantBamboo, which overlaps with groups tracked as Clay Typhoon, UNC5221, and Warp Panda.
  • Volexity discovered in September 2025 that VerdantBamboo had compromised an unnamed victim's Egnyte Storage Sync system by exploiting a local privilege escalation flaw to deploy BRICKSTORM.
  • The attackers used BRICKSTORM's proxying capabilities on the Storage Sync system, along with compromised credentials, to access the victim's Microsoft 365 environment.
  • VerdantBamboo later breached the same organization using stolen administrative credentials to connect to the firewall, configure web SSL VPN access, and deploy additional malware to a Synology NAS appliance.
  • Further investigation found that the threat actor had compromised the victim organization's managed services provider and infected the MSP's pfSense firewall with a BSD variant of BRICKSTORM.

UNC3753 Used Vishing and Physical Intrusions in U.S. Data Theft Extortion Campaign — Must Read

UNC3753 is using vishing, screen-sharing sessions, legitimate remote access tools, and even in-person impersonation of IT staff to steal sensitive data from U.S. legal, financial, and professional services firms. The group moves quickly from initial contact to exfiltration and extortion, often completing the operation within a single business day.

  • Google Mandiant and Google Threat Intelligence Group attributed a financially motivated data theft extortion campaign targeting U.S. professional, legal, and financial services organizations between January and May 2026 to UNC3753.
  • UNC3753 uses vishing and social engineering to pose as IT support, persuade victims to join screen-sharing sessions, and guide them to install legitimate remote monitoring and management tools such as AnyDesk, Bomgar, SuperOps RMM, or Zoho Assist.
  • The FBI reported that UNC3753 has also conducted physical intrusions by posing as IT technicians to enter corporate offices and steal data using external hard drives or USB drives.
  • Stolen data includes proprietary legal agreements, personally identifiable information, financial records, tax filings, audits, corporate client agreements, and Social Security numbers.
  • Google said many UNC3753 operations progressed from initial contact to data extortion within a single business day, with data searches, staging, and theft beginning in under an hour.

Over 20,000 Instagram accounts stolen in Meta AI support hack — Should Read

Meta says attackers abused a flaw in Instagram’s AI-assisted High Touch Support account recovery tool to reset passwords and hijack more than 20,000 accounts that lacked 2FA. Meta disabled the tool, invalidated generated reset links, secured affected accounts, and plans to add proper email verification before relaunching it.

  • Meta disclosed that more than 20,000 Instagram users had their accounts hijacked after attackers abused its AI-assisted High Touch Support account recovery tool to reset passwords.
  • The exploited flaw allowed password reset links to be generated without verifying whether email addresses were associated with the targeted Instagram accounts, enabling account takeovers where two-factor authentication was not enabled.
  • Meta said in a Maine Attorney General data breach filing that it discovered the High Touch Support vulnerability on May 31, 2026, and that 30 users in Maine were potentially affected.
  • Meta disabled the High Touch Support AI-powered support system and all password reset links it generated, then enrolled potentially stolen accounts into a mandatory security checkpoint and required affected users to reset passwords and re-authenticate.
  • Meta said attackers may have accessed contact information, dates of birth, posts, photos, videos, stories, direct messages, account activity, profile information, and linked accounts or services from compromised Instagram accounts.

Microsoft's Own Message Recall Agent Delivered a Credential Harvest — Should Read

A phishing attempt abused Microsoft’s own Message Recall mechanism to generate an internally authenticated recall report that appeared trusted but included a prompt to enter email credentials. The key risk is that system-generated internal messages can bypass normal external email defenses, so detection must inspect content and context rather than relying on authentication alone.

  • Microsoft's Transport Message Recall Routing Agent generated an email authenticated as Internal with AuthAs=Internal and AuthMechanism=05, and the message direction was Originating.
  • The message appeared as a Message Recall Report from Office365Reports@microsoft.com and linked to a legitimate outlook.office.com message recall URL.
  • The email body included the credential prompt, “If prompted, login with your email address and password,” which the article states is not standard language in a Microsoft message recall notification.
  • Themis classified the message at 72% confidence with a VIP Recipient label, citing signals including a first-time sender, high risk, and anomalous community intelligence.
  • Four mailboxes were quarantined after the message was detected.

VS Code Adds 2-Hour Extension Auto-Update Delay to Limit Supply Chain Attacks — Should Read

Microsoft is adding a two-hour delay to automatic VS Code extension updates to reduce exposure to malicious or compromised releases in software supply chain attacks. Users can still manually update immediately, and trusted publishers like Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI are exempt from the delay.

  • Microsoft announced that Visual Studio Code will delay automatic extension updates by two hours to help address software supply chain threats.
  • The delayed automatic update feature is available starting in Visual Studio Code 1.123.
  • Users can still manually update any extension immediately by using the Update button.
  • The two-hour delay does not apply to extensions from trusted publishers such as Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI, which will continue to update immediately.
  • Similar time-based installation delay controls have been added to Bundler, Bun, npm, pnpm, and Yarn.

Oxford University discloses data breach after careers platform hack — May Read

Oxford University disclosed a data breach after third-party provider Group GTI’s CareerConnect platform was hacked, exposing users’ names, email addresses, and encrypted passwords for non-SSO accounts. Oxford says its own systems were not compromised, but affected users may face phishing attempts.

  • Oxford University disclosed a data breach after third-party provider Group GTI informed it that the CareerConnect career services platform had been compromised.
  • Attackers breached the CareerConnect platform on May 28 and accessed users’ first names, last names, email addresses, and encrypted passwords for users who do not sign in using Single Sign-On.
  • Oxford University said there is no evidence that course information, uploaded files, appointment information, financial information, students’ passwords, or university systems were compromised.
  • Group GTI invalidated locally set CareerConnect passwords, and affected alumni, research staff, and employer users will be asked to reset their passwords when they next sign in.
  • Oxford University warned staff, students, and external CareerConnect users that they might be targeted by phishing or scam emails following the incident.

Executive Identity Exposure Has Become a Board-Level Issue — Optional

Executive identities are now high-value targets because attackers can exploit their authority, visibility, and trust for fraud, impersonation, and broader compromise. Organizations should treat executive identity exposure as a board-level business risk and proactively monitor, measure, and reduce it.

  • Attackers increasingly target executives such as CEOs, CFOs, board members, and senior leaders because they possess influence, authority, and trust.
  • Executive identity exposure extends beyond corporate systems to personal email accounts, professional networking platforms, social media profiles, public records, conference appearances, board memberships, and media coverage.
  • Executive impersonation is increasing through tactics such as fake email communications, deepfake audio and video, fraudulent payment requests, and investor and vendor fraud.
  • Artificial intelligence enables attackers to generate highly personalized phishing campaigns, create convincing executive communications, analyze public information at scale, and develop realistic impersonation content.
  • Organizations can manage executive identity risk by using continuous identity monitoring, exposure assessment, executive awareness, incident response planning, and board reporting.
entry.summary: Across this coverage window, reporting centered on identity-driven compromise, software supply chain abuse, and adversaries exploiting trust in legitimate platforms and support workflows. Several incidents involved attackers using valid credentials, recovery mechanisms, third-party services, or...

entry.title: Microsoft Intelligent Terminal Adds AI Assistants to Windows Terminal
entry.content:
This digest window contains a single notable software development item: Microsoft’s open-source Intelligent Terminal, an AI-enabled fork of Windows Terminal. The coverage is not centered on an active threat campaign or incident, but it is relevant to security and IT operations teams because it reflects the continued integration of AI assistants into administrative and developer workflows. Microsoft has created Intelligent Terminal, an open-source fork of Windows Terminal that embeds AI assistance directly inside the terminal experience. The tool is designed to let users interact with an AI assistant in a dedicated Terminal pane without interrupting the regular shell session. According to the report, Intelligent Terminal can explain errors, draft commands, and help users troubleshoot problems without requiring them to leave the command-line environment. On first run, users can select an AI agent for the Terminal pane, with supported options including GitHub Copilot, Claude, Codex, and Gemini. The application includes toggles for automatic error detection, automatic error suggestion, and session management for tracking active and past agent sessions. Intelligent Terminal is available through the Microsoft Store and GitHub, reflecting Microsoft’s continued experimentation with AI-assisted interfaces for technical users.

Hands on with Intelligent Terminal, an AI-powered Windows Terminal — Optional

Microsoft has released Intelligent Terminal, an open-source Windows Terminal fork that embeds AI agents like Claude, Copilot, Codex, and Gemini directly into terminal sessions to explain errors, suggest fixes, and draft commands. Its standout feature is session management, which lets users resume previous AI-assisted terminal work.

  • Microsoft has created an open-source fork of Windows Terminal called Intelligent Terminal that lets users access AI directly inside Terminal without interfering with the regular session.
  • Intelligent Terminal includes a built-in assistant that can explain errors, draft commands, and help fix problems without leaving the terminal.
  • On first run, Intelligent Terminal lets users choose an AI agent for the Terminal pane, including GitHub Copilot, Claude, Codex, and Gemini.
  • The app includes toggles for Automatic error detection, Automatic error suggestion, and Session management to track active and past agent sessions.
  • Intelligent Terminal can be downloaded from the Microsoft Store or GitHub.
entry.summary: This digest window contains a single notable software development item: Microsoft’s open-source Intelligent Terminal, an AI-enabled fork of Windows Terminal. The coverage is not centered on an active threat campaign or incident, but it is relevant to security and IT operations teams because it...

entry.title: C0XMO Botnet Exploits CVE-2021-27137 as Silent Ransom Group Targets Law Firms
entry.content:
This coverage window highlights two distinct but operationally relevant threat streams: router-focused botnet expansion and human-driven data extortion against professional services. The reporting underscores continued attacker reliance on exposed edge devices, weak remote access credentials, social engineering, and legitimate administration tools to gain access and monetize intrusions. Fortinet researchers reported a new Gafgyt variant named C0XMO that targets DD-WRT router firmware through CVE-2021-27137, an unauthenticated buffer overflow vulnerability that can enable arbitrary code execution. The malware supports multiple CPU architectures, including ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, SuperH, x86, and x8664, allowing it to operate across a broad range of embedded and router environments. C0XMO’s infection chain includes deployment of a Python scanner that searches for internet-facing systems, brute-forces weak Telnet and SSH credentials, identifies the target CPU architecture, and installs a compatible malware binary. Once deployed, the botnet copies itself into hidden locations, establishes cron-based persistence, and modifies shell startup files to support automatic execution after reboot or login. The malware also includes competitive botnet suppression behavior, terminating rival malware clients and tools that could interfere with its operation. After installation, C0XMO connects to a hardcoded command-and-control server and can receive commands for distributed denial-of-service activity, with 19 DDoS methods reportedly supported. Mandiant reported that Silent Ransom Group, also tracked as UNC3753, Luna Moth, and Chatty Spider, targeted dozens of legal, financial, and professional services organizations between January and May 2026. The campaign begins with invoice-themed phishing messages sent from consumer email accounts, followed by phone calls in which the attackers impersonate corporate IT personnel. The group’s intrusion workflow relies heavily on social engineering and legitimate remote access channels rather than traditional malware deployment. Victims are persuaded to join remote support sessions through tools such as Microsoft Teams, Zoom, Quick Assist, or Microsoft Terminal Services, after which attackers install remote monitoring and management software including AnyDesk, Zoho Assist, Bomgar, or SuperOps. Once inside victim environments, the attackers search for sensitive legal and financial documents and exfiltrate data using utilities such as WinSCP or Rclone. Mandiant reported that ransom demands often arrive within 30 minutes after the operators leave the environment, with victims typically given three days to respond.

Silent Ransom Group targets law firms with fake IT support calls — Should Read

Silent Ransom Group is targeting U.S. law firms with fake IT support calls that trick employees into granting remote access, enabling attackers to steal sensitive client and financial data within hours. The group now focuses on rapid data-theft extortion rather than ransomware encryption, using aggressive ransom demands and threats to contact clients directly.

  • Mandiant reported that the Silent Ransom Group, tracked as UNC3753, Luna Moth, and Chatty Spider, targeted dozens of legal, financial, and professional services organizations between January and May 2026.
  • The attacks begin with invoice-themed phishing emails from consumer email accounts, followed by phone calls in which attackers impersonate corporate IT staff.
  • Attackers convince employees to join remote support sessions through tools such as Microsoft Teams, Zoom, Quick Assist, or Microsoft Terminal Services and install remote monitoring and management tools such as AnyDesk, Zoho Assist, Bomgar, or SuperOps.
  • Once inside a network, the group searches for sensitive legal and financial documents and exfiltrates data using tools such as WinSCP or Rclone.
  • Mandiant said ransom demands often arrive within 30 minutes of the attackers leaving the victim environment and give organizations three days to respond.

C0XMO botnet spreads via DD-WRT router flaw, kills rival malware — Should Read

A new Gafgyt variant called C0XMO is exploiting a DD-WRT router flaw to infect devices across multiple CPU architectures, spread via scanning and weak SSH/Telnet credentials, and launch DDoS attacks. It also removes rival malware and persistence mechanisms, showing more sophistication than typical IoT botnets.

  • Fortinet researchers discovered a new Gafgyt variant called C0XMO that targets DD-WRT router firmware and supports multiple CPU architectures, including ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, SuperH, x86, and x86_64.
  • C0XMO is delivered by exploiting CVE-2021-27137, an unauthenticated buffer overflow vulnerability that can lead to arbitrary code execution.
  • The botnet downloads a Python scanner that scans internet-facing systems, brute-forces weak Telnet and SSH credentials, detects CPU architecture, and deploys a compatible C0XMO binary.
  • After gaining access, C0XMO copies itself to hidden locations, creates cron jobs for persistence, and modifies shell startup files for automatic execution.
  • C0XMO can terminate competitor botnet clients and interfering tools, then connects to a hardcoded command-and-control server to receive commands including DDoS attacks using 19 supported methods.
entry.summary: This coverage window highlights two distinct but operationally relevant threat streams: router-focused botnet expansion and human-driven data extortion against professional services. The reporting underscores continued attacker reliance on exposed edge devices, weak remote access credentials,...

entry.title: NetSuite Invoice Phishing and Apache Solr CVE-2026-44825
entry.content:
This coverage window highlights two distinct but related security themes: adversary abuse of trusted business infrastructure and exposure created by authentication weaknesses in enterprise platforms. The reported activity shows that technically valid authentication signals, whether email authentication or application login controls, can still coexist with high-impact risk when trust relationships or default credentials are misused. IRONSCALES reported an invoice phishing email delivered through legitimate Oracle NetSuite infrastructure, using the sender system@sent-via[.]netsuite[.]com on behalf of “Finance QIMA/WQS.” The message was relayed through Oracle Email Delivery from a Japan datacenter and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks, demonstrating how phishing can evade controls that rely heavily on sender authentication and infrastructure reputation. The phishing lure used a “Click and Pay” call-to-action that directed recipients to a real QIMA payment portal containing a pre-filled payment token. This made the message appear aligned with normal invoice-payment workflows while shifting the risk to the embedded payment token and business-process context rather than an obviously malicious domain or attachment. Microsoft assigned the message SCL=9 with SFTY:9.25 despite successful authentication, indicating that downstream content and behavioral signals still flagged the email as highly suspicious. Themis classified the message as Invoice Phishing with 63% confidence based on sender relationship analysis and quarantined it across four mailboxes, underscoring the role of relationship and workflow context in detecting abuse of legitimate SaaS platforms. CyCognito detailed CVE-2026-44825, a hardcoded credentials vulnerability affecting Apache Solr’s Basic Authentication setup tool, bin/solr auth enable. The flaw has a CVSS v3.1 base score of 8.1 and affects Apache Solr versions 9.4.0 through 9.10.1 and version 10.0.0 in SolrCloud deployments where Basic Authentication was enabled using the affected tool. The issue arises when the setup process writes a security.json file containing template accounts named superadmin, admin, search, and index, each with a password matching the username. In affected deployments, authentication as superadmin can provide full administrative control over the SolrCloud cluster, including the ability to read, modify, or delete indexes and alter cluster configuration. Apache released Solr 9.11.0 and 10.1.0 to address CVE-2026-44825. The reporting also notes that organizations unable to immediately upgrade can identify exposure by examining security.json for the template accounts, making the vulnerability particularly relevant to internet-exposed or broadly accessible SolrCloud environments where administrative authentication is reachable.

Emerging Threat: (CVE-2026-44825) Apache Solr Administrative Takeover via Hardcoded Credentials — Should Read

Apache Solr versions 9.4.0 through 9.10.1 and 10.0.0 may silently create default admin accounts when BasicAuth is enabled with bin/solr auth enable, allowing unauthenticated attackers to gain full SolrCloud administrative access. Organizations should upgrade to Solr 9.11.0 or 10.1.0 and audit security.json for default accounts such as superadmin, admin, search, and index.

  • CVE-2026-44825 is a hardcoded credentials vulnerability in Apache Solr’s Basic Authentication setup tool, bin/solr auth enable, with a CVSS v3.1 base score of 8.1.
  • When administrators use bin/solr auth enable, the tool may write a security.json file containing template accounts named superadmin, admin, search, and index, each with a password matching the username.
  • An attacker who authenticates as superadmin can gain full administrative control of the SolrCloud cluster, including the ability to read, modify, or delete indexes and alter cluster configuration.
  • Apache Solr versions 9.4.0 through 9.10.1 and version 10.0.0 are affected in SolrCloud deployments where Basic Authentication was enabled using bin/solr auth enable.
  • Apache has released Solr 9.11.0 and 10.1.0 to address CVE-2026-44825, and organizations should audit security.json for the template accounts if immediate upgrading is not possible.

NetSuite Sent the Invoice. Oracle Signed It. The Payment Token Was the Weapon. — Should Read

A phishing invoice was sent through legitimate Oracle NetSuite infrastructure with valid SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, linking to a real QIMA payment portal with a pre-filled payment token. The case shows that authenticated SaaS-generated emails can still be fraudulent, making behavioral analysis critical for detecting invoice scams.

  • An invoice phishing email was delivered through legitimate Oracle NetSuite infrastructure and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication checks.
  • The email was sent from system@sent-via[.]netsuite[.]com on behalf of “Finance QIMA/WQS” and relayed through Oracle Email Delivery from a Japan datacenter.
  • The “Click and Pay” call-to-action linked to a real QIMA payment portal containing a pre-filled payment token.
  • Microsoft assigned the message SCL=9 with SFTY:9.25 despite the email passing authentication.
  • Themis classified the message as Invoice Phishing at 63% confidence based on sender relationship analysis and quarantined it across four mailboxes.
entry.summary: This coverage window highlights two distinct but related security themes: adversary abuse of trusted business infrastructure and exposure created by authentication weaknesses in enterprise platforms. The reported activity shows that technically valid authentication signals, whether email...
